speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 5 with Erwin Chaim, October 25, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. We arrived in Minsk and were fed by the Jewish population who came to the train. Unfortunately, we were so undernourished and could not take the richness of the food. And I remember, well, throwing up most of the food. We went on and arrived in Moscow. In Moscow, we were met by the-- what the Russians call the [NON-ENGLISH]. Basically what these are is these were our, our-- well, our guards, basically is what they were. We went to a hotel, the Hotel Metropol, one of the finer hotels. This was all paid for beforehand by my father. I know that there were some other refugees, some other Jews, with us that were in that same hotel. I also remember that there was, I would say, a couple of hundred, at least a couple of hundred, other Jews on that train. If they all stayed in that same hotel, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before-- I don't mean to interrupt you-- before we started this tape, you had said your father wanted to see. Did he get off the train and look in Minsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Minsk, my father, being in the German army, and after he lost his hearing in one of his ears, he was declared not being able to fight anymore. And then he was degraded, shall we say, of guarding prisoners, Russian prisoners, at that time, in Minsk. He wanted to see what Minsk looked like. The station that we were-- where we arrived was a very fancy stone buildings, nicely painted. But the minute he got out of the station and one block away was just as tiny, just as unkempt as it was in 1917. That's what he told us later. He roamed the streets a little bit, tried to find some landmarks. I don't think he did. But anyway, he had an adventure there and roamed around Minsk for maybe half an hour. We had quite a layover in Minsk, by the way, maybe three or four hours. I forgot to mention-- when we arrived in-- at the station in Moscow, we saw our luggage on the platform in the Moscow station. These great, big, huge stevedore trunks were at the station. They all had numbers on them, our numbers that we painted and with our initials-- HC, Henry Chaim. And most of the trunks and suitcases were upside down. Don't know why. Maybe the Russians can't read. They were all upside down. OK. We went to the hotel and were informed that we are going to be delayed in Moscow for, I think, four days, because we are waiting for the arrival of another transport of Jewish refugees from Germany. And they want to combine the two into one to go all the way through Russia into Siberia and then on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we spent, I would say, an enjoyable time in Moscow at the time. This is in 1940, October-- September, October. I don't know the correct date. And I remember riding the subways, which are beautiful. Each station is a work of art. You were allowed to travel wherever you wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the company of some of the [NON-ENGLISH], the guards. We also went to an agricultural exhibit, a fair. I also remember not being able to communicate with the Russian peoples at all. We didn't speak Russian, and they didn't speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just German at that time, just German. Our family did not speak any Yiddish, and I also was not allowed to speak the Berlin slang. This was not the thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we finally left Russia-- excuse me-- Moscow, went on through Russia monotonously, had some fair amount of food. And as an aside, we did have some provisions with us, bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we were on the train, and after we were in Russia, by this time already, the second week, that bread was just as fresh as it was the first day we bought it. The reason was there really wasn't much wheat or flour in it. It was mostly sawdust. Yes, sawdust. By that time, we were already able to buy some of the better bread that the Russians had at that time. We went through Russia into Siberia around the Baikal Sea, which is a rather-- one of the largest indoor-- surrounded by land-- seas. Arrived at the border between Russia and Manchuria. Manchuria, at that time, was occupied by the Japanese. The Japanese took Manchuria away from the Russian during the Russian-Japanese War in 1902, I believe. At the border, we were met by the Japanese Garrison who lined us up-- and I would say a couple of hundred peoples-- searched us, and took away our watches and our cigarette lighters. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe some other things too. I don't remember. Some people on the train complained about that to whoever was in charge. The next day, all of our belongings were returned to us. Absolutely. We were chauffeured in trucks, I believe, to the Jewish Community Center of-- no, excuse me-- the border between Russia and Manchuria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I, I don't know if we stayed there. I don't think so. No, no, no, no. We were herded back on the train. We were herded back on the train. Yes, yes, yes. I remember now what happened. We were herded back on the train, and we left. Erwin over here, who's always been a train fan, had to investigate the station, just like my father. And I didn't hear the train whistle blow. And the train started to move, and I was outside. I was on the station platform. And the doors were closed. The train was going very slowly. I kept running towards the front of the train and finally, somebody pulled me in. Otherwise, maybe I would have still been left in Manchuria. OK. We went through Manchuria and came to the Port of Dalian. This is where we went to-- where a ship-- by truck-- to the Jewish Community Center. And we stayed in Dalian about two or three days to await the arrival of a ship that took us through the China Sea into Shanghai. Again, all of our tickets, all of our fares were booked in advance. We had first-class cabins on this small steamer. Well, what happened was, there were some Nazis on board that ship. And they said, forget it. Nobody goes to first class. Those Jews, they go down in the hold. Like cattle pens is what they were. And that's where we had to go. The first meal, I remember, was served on us on metal dishes. It was like slop. And my father threw it into the cook's face. And this was also the first time that I saw my mother and father hugging each other, breaking down, and crying. I, I think that was the first indication what-- from things to come, of what China is like. This was a Chinese boat, and it was already pretty dirty. We, we, we could not stand the stench that went on down there. We were only there overnight, but it was still pretty bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say there were 400 of us by that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Down in the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Down in the-- they were on the ice mats, like pens. So my father, my sister, and my mother, and I, we went upstairs to, well, on deck, actually, and stayed on deck all night long and slept in a chair.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were allowed to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed there. I don't know if we were allowed to or not, but that's where we were. And apparently, nobody, nobody chased us.

speaker: interviewer
text: We got to the Shanghai Harbor. We were met at the Shanghai Harbor by the Jewish committee who, again, herded us onto trucks. Well, my father being a dentist and being upper-middle class, we were not used to be treated like cattle, you know? But that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the humanity, the density of the population-- we were used to a certain amount of space. The Chinese don't know that. They are like that. And we were herded-- well, we were transported to Hongkou-- Hongkou was the district that most of the Jewish refugees lived in-- by the committee. And if you had any relatives over there who made arrangements for you to stay someplace, you were packed into the [NON-ENGLISH], into the homes, which were converted schools where quite a number of the refugees stayed between eight and 10 years. Some of the refugees already came in 1936, 1937. Those were large halls where a hundred people at a time slept in bunks two-high, with the only privacy that they had between families of a sheet. These were the people who were unable to make any kind of a living in Shanghai.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since you had relatives that brought you over there, did they have a place for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a place for us to stay. And like I mentioned before, that particular place was on the ground floor. And unfortunately, a typhoon, a hurricane, hit the first or second day we were there. The apartment-- ah, the apartment-- the room was flooded. All the dirty water and everything, and the filth flooded the floor to about a foot deep. And we knew that we couldn't stay in this particular place. And we-- my father, being very energetic and a man who can take charge, found us a two-room and kitchen apartment on the ground floor in what was called-- it still is called, [NON-ENGLISH]. This is like a housing development. It was brand new. We were the first people in there, but it already had bedbugs. Those are built into the walls. We stayed in this particular place only, maybe, less than a year. And we were able to secure an apartment at 53 [NON-ENGLISH] Road. Those were called mission houses. They were made out of real stone and wood. They were three stories high, and they were, for Chinese standards, beautiful. They were made in the European fashion. This is where we lived the rest of the, shall we say, six years, in one room and a porch, an enclosed porch. The room was, basically, really, no more than, maybe, 20 feet by 20 feet. This is where we lived. This is where my father practiced his dentistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was able to do that. He worked mostly with Japanese, which was very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Hanz? My uncle escaped out of Germany, and I do not know if it was legally or illegally. He was arrested at the border. The border, at that time, where he tried to get across was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was thrown in jail. My uncle, having claustrophobia-- whoa-- went wild. Some sympathetic person called my mother, my uncle's brother, and says, hey, you gotta come over here. You gotta get this guy released. Well, my father went to our family doctor, got a certificate that my, my, my uncle was buried alive during the First World War and has claustrophobia. And he's got to get into a hospital. Only my mother went. She was able to get the release of my, my uncle, and he had to go to a hospital. He was-- he was out of it. And he then left. He went to Bolivia. From Bolivia, he tried to get into Chile illegally. There is a-- the Titicaca, the Titicaca Lake. It's one of the highest lakes in the world. He went across the border in a small rowboat into, well, ended up in Santiago, Santiago de Chile. And in Santiago de Chile, he opened up his same profession that he had in, in Germany. He became a very respected person, making shirts to orders, again, to the higher-class people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep in touch with him? Did you know-- or how did he know where you were? Or how did you know where he was? [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were ways through the Red Cross and through South Africa to get mail from one place to another. Usually, it took like two or three months to get mail from one place to another. Yes, we knew where he was. And he knew, knew where we were. To continue on my, my, my uncle for just a second here-- what happened to him, he was actually quite happy, made a good living. And after the war and everything was over, came to visit us in-- here in Denver, Colorado and decided that Santiago is getting on the communistic side. He needed to get out of there. Actually closed his business in Santiago and wanted to open his business again, of all places, in Hollywood. Because he was used to this kind of clientele, the actors, the actresses, and all that. Well, he tried, and it was absolutely no way. No way. He tried, actually, here in Denver to also open that. Didn't work. He stayed here roughly about half a year, decided that it was not possible for him to make a living over here. Only other way to do is go back to Santiago. He went back to Santiago, was able to buy back most of his very specialized sewing machines, and was in business again for a very short time. The communists took over, took his business away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By that time, he was old enough to be eligible for social security. Social security from-- not from Santiago, social security from Germany. So he decided, I've had enough. I'm going back to Germany. The incentive being that you can get a partial social security if you do not live in Germany. If you move back to Germany, you will get the full Social Security. So that's what my uncle did. He did not go back to Berlin. Apparently, Berlin held too many bad memories for him. He went to another very nice place, Baden-Baden. Baden-Baden is a resort for the rich. And he lived within a stone's throw of the Baden-Baden casino. I did visit him in 1971, and he had a very nice apartment with a balcony. Did not really work anymore in Germany. Matter of fact, he was quite sick. He had a heart ailment, and that was the only time that I saw my uncle after he fled out of Germany. And a year later, he passed away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was an aside for my uncle. Where were we? When-- we were in Shanghai? When you moved into your quarter--

speaker: interviewee
text: Into 53 [NON-ENGLISH] Road and lived in that one room for, for all that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue with your schooling?

speaker: interviewee
text: We continued with schooling. Shanghai was also a haven for a lot of what was called the White Russians and some of the Jews that were wealthy enough not to be communist during the take over from the czarist period. And a lot of them fled into Shanghai. And some of them were-- had, had large holdings. And one of the richer Jews of Shanghai was named [? Kadori. ?] I don't remember his first name. And he opened up a school for Jewish refugees in our district. The school was all in English. My sister had absolutely no trouble whatsoever in the school learning the language real fast and keeping up with the curriculum. Not me. I was not able to keep up with the curriculum and learn English at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents took me to another school, the ORT school. Which the ORT organization is famous all around the world of helping Jews who were lawyers, doctors, and dentists to immigrate to Palestine at that time and learn the trade that is used to more advantage in Israel, farming, carpentry, locksmithing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I did in Shanghai. I went to school in the morning, and I learned carpentry, electricity, and locksmithing, and spray painting in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language was your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The language was partially in, in German and partially in English. So I eased into this much, much better. That was the school I went to. And after the war was over and Shanghai became liberated, I actually used my, my skills and became a carpenter for the American forces. And I also did some spray painting. And that was my first job that I had when I came to Denver.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you got to Denver, were you aware-- you were still young. I think you were what? Around 12? Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware, or were your parents aware, of what was going on in Europe--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was known that, of course, things were bad. You had your business taken away from you. You got beaten up. People disappeared. People were put into work camps. People were interrogated and tortured and were never the same afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the idea of the death camps really did not-- was not known until 1944 or '45 when it became known and everybody denied it. How can such a thing happen? So no, no, no.l We did not know, in Shanghai, what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did happen, of course, was the fact that yes, we were allowed to come to Shanghai, the occupied part by the Japanese. We were allowed to come to Shanghai by the Japanese-- not the Chinese, the Japanese-- with most of us declaring our intentions of coming to the United States as early as whenever it is possible. And matter of fact, we have to thank my mother for that. That she was so absolutely devastated by having to stay in Shanghai for who knows how many years, that she immediately-- I mean, the second or third week that we were able to get around in Shanghai-- went to the American Council and got a number, a waiting number, to be put on the waiting list to come to the United States whenever possible. The idea being that you take out a waiting list number, and you already, having in your possession documents, resource, permission to come to United States, which we didn't have. But she took out a number anyway, which stood us in very good deed when the war was over. We were called two years after the war was over in 1947. When we were called to come present our documents, we didn't have any. So we sent a telegram to our very good friends, the Michaelsons, who were both friends of my father and my mother in Berlin who were able to immigrate to the United States and to Denver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, help us. This is an emergency. We have a number. We have been called-- oh, I don't know, maybe two or three weeks in advance. I don't remember. Can you help us? Can you get us some documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Michelson went-- he being not rich enough to guarantee the American government that we would not become a burden-- went to a senator, Hershfield of Hershfield Press. He was a former senator of the Colorado government. And he said, yes, I will help you. I will send the telegram that the visa is on the way. And, of course, he signed it, Senator Abraham Hershfield, Denver, Colorado. We did not have any trouble whatsoever with anything of leaving Shanghai after that. When a senator signs your affidavit, yes-- [LAUGHS] --you get the papers. Now, of course, the Shanghai ghetto-- between 18 and 20,000 people. We lived together and formed a city, a community, a community of our own with a lot of the people being able to make a living, having factories, having stores. We were having theaters. We had cafes. We had synagogues in the rooms. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What we did have-- we had too many lawyers, we had too many doctors, and we had too many dentists. My father was one of the dentists that, fortunately enough, that he was able to build up a clientele of Japanese. He was able to make some sort of a living, still not enough to really make a living. I stood in line twice a day to get us some hot food from a central kitchen. My father went and started selling some of our goods. Being that we were one of the last people to get into Shanghai, we had quite a number of very sought after items. My father always believed, don't buy one. Buy a dozen. So the dental equipment that he brought over, we had a lot of teeth-- false teeth-- which were non-existent in Shanghai. We had a lot of amalgam fillings and some other things. That was some of the stuff that we were able-- that weren't confiscated.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's change tapes right here.
